core_identity.title: Vectors: The Language of Direction and Magnitude
core_identity.author: Gerry Docherty

curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.subtopic: vector notation, vector addition, and scalar multiplication
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Represent movement using vectors (magnitude and direction), combine vectors by addition, and scale vectors using scalar multiplication, connecting the maths to navigation and real movement.

verse_ality.driving_question: How can vectors describe movement and position with both direction and magnitude — and what happens when we add and scale them?

knowing_world.focus: Introduce vectors as quantities with magnitude and direction, and explore how components encode movement so we can model translations, forces, and velocities precisely.

human_story.focus: Connect vectors to everyday and professional contexts where direction and size matter, including navigation, engineering, sport trajectories, and GPS-based movement.
justice_prompts: Where do you encounter directional movement in your everyday life?

expressive_self.focus: Use play and creativity to experiment with combining vectors and notice how changing magnitude or direction changes the overall motion and patterns you can create.
activities: Use the interactive tool to create your own vector combinations and explore the patterns they make.
Change the magnitude or direction of a vector and describe how the resultant changes.
Create a movement pattern (path) using multiple vectors and describe it in words.

designed_world.focus: Use the tool to model and visualise vector operations so abstract ideas (addition and scaling) become concrete, supporting accurate design thinking in movement and translation problems.
design_challenges: Plot individual vectors and label their components clearly.
Visualise vector addition and compare your prediction to the tool’s resultant.
Explore scalar multiplication: test what changes and what stays the same when you multiply by different scalars.
Explain how the visual representation helps you understand vector operations more clearly.

wellbeing_prompts: Describe your own movement using vectors: how would you represent walking 3 steps east and 4 steps north?

pathways.name: Final Task: Vector Addition and Closure
pathways.focus: Create two or more vectors, predict their sum, and test your prediction using the interactive tool; then explore whether you can form a closed vector triangle and explain what it means.
products: Two or more vectors created in the interactive tool (screenshot or sketch).
A prediction of the resultant vector (direction and magnitude/components).
Checked result using the tool, with a short comparison to your prediction.
A closed vector triangle attempt and an explanation of what it shows about the relationship between vectors.

required: Comfort with coordinates or grid movement (horizontal/vertical steps)
Basic understanding of direction (e.g., compass directions)

builds_toward: Transformations as translations on a coordinate grid
Velocity and displacement in graphs and motion contexts
Further vector geometry (collinearity and parallel vectors)
